question_structure.context: In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.
question_structure.sub_question_1: The magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
question_structure.sub_question_2: The position of the ion-implanted wafer, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system, is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.
question_structure.sub_question_3: The position where ions are implanted on the wafer when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.
question_structure.sub_question_4: Determine the position where the ions are implanted on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

sub_question_1.step_1: The velocity of the ions passing through the velocity selector is given by $v=\frac{E}{B}$.
sub_question_1.step_2: The radius of the circular motion of the ions emitted from the center aperture $N$ of the magnetic analyzer is $R=\frac{R_{1}+R_{2}}{2}$.

sub_question_2.step_4: After passing through the electric field, the distance the ions are deflected in the $x$ direction is $x_{1}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{q E}{m}(\frac{L}{v})^{2}$.
sub_question_2.step_5: The deflection angle $\theta$ of the ions after leaving the electric field is $\tan\theta={\frac{q E L}{mv^{2}}}$.
sub_question_2.step_6: The offset distance $x_2$ of the ions in the $x$ direction after leaving the electric field is $x_{2}=L\tan\theta={\frac{q E L^{2}}{mv^{2}}}$.
sub_question_2.step_7: The total offset distance $x$ is given by $x=x_{1}+x_{2}={\frac{3q E L^{2}}{2mv^{2}}}={\frac{3L^{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}}$.
sub_question_2.step_8: Therefore, the position coordinates where the ions are implanted onto the wafer are $(\frac{3L^{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}, 0)$.

sub_question_3.step_9: The radius of the circular motion of the ions after entering the magnetic field is $r=\frac{mv}{q B}$.
sub_question_3.step_10: The deflection angle $\alpha$ is given by $\sin\alpha={\frac{L}{r}}$.
sub_question_3.step_11: After passing through the magnetic field, the deflection distance of the ions in the y direction is $y_{1}=r(1-\cos\alpha) \approx \frac{L^{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}$.
sub_question_3.step_12: After leaving the magnetic field, the offset distance $y_2$ of the ions in the $y$ direction is $y_{2}=L\tan\alpha \approx {\frac{2L^{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}}$.

sub_question_4.step_15: The position coordinates where the ions are implanted onto the wafer are $(\frac{3L^{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}, \frac{3L^{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}})$, and the velocity increment caused by the electric field does not affect the motion in the y-direction.

step_1.physical_theorem: Balance of electric and magnetic forces in velocity selector

result_quantity.name: Velocity of ions after passing through the velocity selector

step_2.physical_theorem: Definition of the radius of a circle

result_quantity.name: Radius of the circular motion of ions in the magnetic analyzer
Charge-to-mass ratio of ions selected by the magnetic analyzer

step_4.physical_theorem: Motion of a charged particle in a uniform electric field

result_quantity.name: Deflection distance in the x direction due to electric field

step_5.physical_theorem: Relationship between velocity and deflection angle of charged particle in electric field

result_quantity.name: Deflection angle of ions after leaving the electric field
Offset distance in the x direction after leaving the electric field
Total offset distance in the x direction
Position coordinates of ion implantation on the wafer (only electric field)
Radius of the circular motion of ions in the magnetic field
Deflection angle of ions in the magnetic field
Deflection distance in the y direction due to magnetic field
Offset distance in the y direction after leaving the magnetic field
Total offset distance in the y direction
Position coordinates of ion implantation on the wafer (only magnetic field)

step_15.physical_theorem: Superposition of electric and magnetic fields, and independence of motion in orthogonal directions

result_quantity.name: Position coordinates of ion implantation on the wafer (both electric and magnetic fields)

image_captions:
The image depicts a mass spectrometry system used for ion beam focusing and delivery onto a wafer. The system starts with an ion source, followed by a velocity selector, a magnetic analyzer, a deflection system, and finally the wafer.

The velocity selector utilizes perpendicular electric (E) and magnetic (B) fields to filter out ions with a specific velocity. The selected ions then enter the magnetic analyzer, where a perpendicular magnetic field (B) causes them to move in a curved path. Ions with different mass-to-charge ratios will have different radii of curvature (R1, R2). After the magnetic analyzer, the ions pass through a deflection system with perpendicular electric (E) and magnetic (B) fields. This system controls the final direction of the ions before they land on the wafer at position O along the x-y axes.
